figures.caption: Fig. 1: Examples of calculating success metric. In B1K only the final state of the objects are considered to measure success. (Top) three objects are in the cabinet but the cabinet is not closed, hence Q-score is 0.75. Our safety Q-score (sQ), penalizes the score since one appliance was dropped (target violated (TV)) and damaged. (Bottom), three objects are properly placed in the box and fridge is closed, hence Q-score and sQ are 0.67. Our safety enhanced Q-score (seQ) adds two additional sub-goals for non-targets, the box and chopping board. Since the board is dropped (non-target violated (nTV)), seQ is 0.63.

TABLE I: Results of the top 2 models reruns on the B1K Challenge reported using the Q-score (Q) and proposed metrics. Task names are shortened for brevity. Red means the score reduction occurred only due to target violations (TV) and blue means non-target violations (nTV) contributed to score reduction. The direction of arrows shows higher or lower is better.
Fig. 6: Qualitative examples of safety violations that resulted in score penalty.
Fig. 2: The posted results of the RLC policy and reproduced ones using the officially released checkpoints. The values show large disparity between results.
Fig. 7: Total number of one occurrence of the failure among all tasks.
Fig. 4: Different types of errors occurred in tasks. The graph only shows one occurrence of the failure per task.
Fig. 3: Performance of RLC per task, showing the Q-score across different trials. The tasks are sorted, in an ascending order based on the average Q-score from left to right.
Fig. 5: Qualitative examples of failures of the RLC policy that lead to unsuccessful attempts in B1K.
